output_text:
**Summary:** Critique of the related-work/intro section, flagging factual errors, overstated novelty claims, and missing methodological detail.

---

**Major issues**

1. **Hannun et al. (2014) is not "the most recent confidence-aware ASR model."** That citation is Deep Speech ("Deep Speech: Scaling up end-to-end speech recognition") — an 11-year-old baseline ASR paper, not a recent confidence-calibration method. Calling a 2014 paper "most recent" in a submission dated 2025/2026 will be flagged immediately by any ASR reviewer. There is a substantial post-2017 literature on ASR confidence (e.g., Li et al. on confidence estimation for end-to-end ASR, Kumar et al., Qiu et al., the various Google/Amazon utterance-level confidence papers, and entropy/MC-dropout/learned-confidence approaches in the late 2010s and early 2020s). Replace this baseline with something actually recent, or drop the "most recent" framing.

2. **Hannun et al. (2014) is also mischaracterized.** Deep Speech is not primarily "a CTC decoder with an entropy regularizer on the output distribution." If you mean entropy-regularized CTC, that's a different line of work (e.g., Liu et al.'s entropy-regularized CTC, or maximum-entropy CTC variants). Verify the actual baseline and cite it correctly.

3. **"First systematic study of token-level recalibration in modern transformer ASR encoders"** is too strong. Token/frame-level recalibration in end-to-end ASR has been studied — at minimum, Kumar et al. on word-level confidence for end-to-end ASR and Google's recent work on calibration of attention-based ASR. Either tighten the claim ("first to apply X-specific technique to Y") or back it with an explicit literature sweep.

4. **Guo et al. (2017) as the "de facto current standard"** undercuts your own novelty pitch. Temperature scaling has been extended many times (Platt scaling variants, vector/matrix scaling, Dirichlet calibration, focal-loss calibration of Mukhoti et al. 2020, etc.). State why temperature scaling specifically is the right anchor here, rather than implying nothing has happened since 2017.

**Methodological gaps**

5. **ECE numbers are unaccompanied by the binning scheme.** Specify number of bins, equal-width vs. equal-mass, and whether ECE is computed over tokens, words, or utterances. ECE is notoriously sensitive to these choices — 3.1% vs. 5.8% is meaningless without them. Also report adaptive ECE or Brier score as a sanity check.

6. **"WER regression below 0.1 absolute" needs the absolute WER.** Going from 4.0 → 4.1 is very different from 15.0 → 15.1 in practical terms.

7. **"Comparable WER" on test-other is hand-wavy** — give the number.

8. **Train/dev/test contamination check:** you train the recalibration head on dev-other and report ECE on test-other. That's fine, but say explicitly that dev-other and test-other have disjoint speakers (they do in LibriSpeech, but reviewers will check).

9. **Figure 3 reliability-diagram claim about the ≥0.9 bin** needs either confidence intervals or per-bin sample counts. The top bin in ASR token distributions is enormous and most ECE mass often lives there mechanically; the framing should acknowledge that.

**Framing**

10. **The tool-use motivation is asserted but not connected.** You open with "spoken tool-use agents" and close by tying to Park et al. (2025) / Yamamoto et al. (2025), but the body of the section is pure ASR calibration. Either add one sentence showing how token-level ASR confidence feeds an agent's tool-routing decision, or move the agentic framing out of this section — right now it reads as topical decoration.

11. **"Posteriors that match empirical correctness — when the system says 90%, it should be right 90% of the time"** is the textbook definition of calibration. Fine, but if you're targeting a venue where reviewers know this, consider compressing.

**Minor**

- "the most recent confidence-aware ASR model of Hannun et al. (2014)" — internal contradiction in a single clause.
- "current standard … following Guo et al. (2017)" — "current … following [8-year-old paper]" reads oddly.
- "We position this as the first systematic study" — passive-novelty phrasing; reviewers parse this as "we couldn't find prior work but didn't look hard."

---

**Uncertainty disclosure:** I have not pulled the Hannun et al. (2014) paper or the Park/Yamamoto 2025 references to verify them line-by-line — my characterization of Hannun 2014 as Deep Speech is from memory and should be confirmed. The post-2017 ASR-confidence literature I cite (Li, Kumar, Qiu, Mukhoti) is mentioned from recall; check exact titles before relying on them as baselines.
